Qualifies as SM variable paired with individual-trait outcome.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1 identifies qualifying phone/SM-specific variable: "Social media time (hours per day) trajectories" with three trajectory groups measured longitudinally. Three groups: no/very low (57.6%, +0.3 h/d by age 13), low increasing (36.6%, +1.3 h/d), high increasing (5.8%, +3.0 h/d). Multiple regression results show social media trajectories paired with cognitive outcomes: Low increasing SM use associated with lower Oral Reading Recognition Test (ORRT) score difference −1.39 (95% CI, −2.10 to −0.68), Picture Sequence Memory Test (PSMT) −2.03 (95% CI, −2.91 to −1.15), Picture Vocabulary Test (PVT) −2.09 (95% CI, −2.78 to −1.41), total composite −0.85 (95% CI, −1.30 to −0.40); all p < .001. High increasing use: ORRT −1.68, PSMT −4.51, PVT −3.85, composite −1.76. Other side: Cognitive performance measured via NIH Toolbox (reading, memory, vocabulary, processing speed, composite) are individual-trait-level cognitive outcomes.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with robust standard errors examining associations between three trajectory groups (no/very low, low increasing, high increasing social media use) and year 2 cognitive performance across five cognitive subtests and composite score.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: adjusted mean differences with 95% confidence intervals for each cognitive subtest across three trajectory groups (Table); p-values ranging from <.001 to .33; regression coefficients with standard errors for all analyses.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Comprehensive numeric results reported: adjusted mean differences (95% CI) for all cognitive measures across trajectory groups. Examples: low increasing social media use vs reference: ORRT -1.39 (95% CI, -2.10 to -0.68, p < .001), PSMT -2.03 (95% CI, -2.91 to -1.15, p < .001), composite -0.85 (95% CI, -1.30 to -0.40, p < .001). High increasing: ORRT -1.68 (95% CI, -3.08 to -0.28, p = .02), PSMT -4.51 (95% CI, -6.48 to -2.54, p < .001), composite -1.76 (95% CI, -2.72 to -0.81, p < .001).
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Same numeric results as FT-IC3; multiple quantitative point estimates (adjusted mean differences) and confidence intervals reported for SM trajectory × cognitive outcome associations.
FT_EC7_applies_C1_evidence: Social media use is IV (predictor) in primary analyses; EC7 requires SM-as-DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere in the paper. This paper explicitly uses SM trajectories to predict cognitive outcomes.

Other side: baseline individual differences in impulsivity (urgency, sensation-seeking, lack of planning, lack of perseverance), motivation (reward sensitivity, punishment sensitivity), psychopathology (internalizing, externalizing), and cognitive ability (general cognitive ability, executive functioning, learning/memory) are all individual-trait-level variables.

FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric results for relationships between individual-trait variables and phone/SM use: β estimates, standard errors, p-values throughout. Examples: impulsivity urgency→video game ST (β = +0.07), urgency→smartphone PSU (MPIQ), reward sensitivity→VGAQ (β higher at year 4).
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric results with β coefficients, standard errors, and p-values for all SM↔individual-trait associations in Tables 3 and 4.
FT_EC7_applies_C1_evidence: Design is cross-sectional at baseline (ages 9-10 predicting year 4 ages 12-15) with SM/phone time and PSU as dependent variables; no bidirectional or SM-as-IV analysis. EC7 would apply only if all three conditions held: longitudinal, SM always DV, no SM-as-IV anywhere. FT_EC7_applies_C2_evidence: All three FT-EC7 conditions hold: (a) design is longitudinal (baseline at ages 9-10 predicts screen use at year 4, ages 12-15); (b) smartphone/SM use (ST and PSU) is the DV in every analysis; (c) there is no analysis anywhere in the paper with SM as IV (individual differences are IVs, SM is always DV). FT_IC1_C2_evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, N=9,538 adolescents (9-10 years at baseline 2016-2018) with 2 years follow-up, 21 recruitment sites.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis; mixed-effects models analyzing associations between baseline self-reported screen time and parent-reported mental health symptoms (Child Behavior Checklist) with two-year longitudinal follow-up.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Prospective cohort analysis (baseline to Year 2 follow-up) using mixed-effects models to examine associations between baseline self-reported screen time and parent-reported mental health symptoms on Child Behavior Checklist.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed published journal article in BMC Public Health (2024).
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed original empirical publication in BMC Public Health.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate isolated with its own coefficient. Methods state screen time includes "television shows, movies, videos, video chat, single and multi-player video games, texting, video chatting (e.g., Skype, Facetime), and social media (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Twitter)." Results report associations for total screen time only (e.g., β=0.10 for depressive symptoms, β=0.07 for conduct symptoms). Text mentions "video chat, texting, videos, and video games" had greatest associations but no separate numeric estimates (no table showing per-modality coefficients). Step 2 composite-content check: Composite includes TV, movies, video streaming (non-phone device), multi-player gaming (platform unspecified), texting, video chat, and social media. Non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV, movies, streaming, unspecified gaming) are bundled without separate breakout in reported results tables (Table 2 shows only aggregate screen time models).
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1: No isolated phone/SM-specific coefficient found in primary results. Main results table (Table 2) reports only continuous aggregate "total screen time" (B=0.10, 95% CI 0.06, 0.13) and categorical screen time (0 to 4 h, >4-8, >8 h). Text mentions "video chat, texting, videos, and video games" had greatest associations, but these modality-specific estimates are not reported with separate coefficients in primary analyses. Step 2: Composite structure includes TV, movies, video streaming (non-phone), video chat, texting, video games (platform unspecified), and social media as aggregate. "Videos" and "video games" without platform specification fail criteria (gaming alone without explicit phone/mobile platform specification fails FT-IC2). Composite bundles non-phone/non-SM (TV, movies, unspecified gaming) with phone/SM without per-item breakout → NOT_MET.

FT_IC1_C2_evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, participants aged 9-10 at baseline, N = 9,474, longitudinal data 2016-2022
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis; group-based trajectory modeling of screen time/social media patterns; multivariable linear models examining anxiety/depression as outcomes
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis: group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) for screen time and social media patterns; multivariable linear regression examining associations with anxiety/depression
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in BMC Public Health 26:262, 2026
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article, BMC Public Health, 2026
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Step 1 failure: No per-modality phone/SM-specific estimate found in results locations. Methods state screen time aggregated across "electronic devices (e.g., TV, texting, gaming, social media)" without separate phone/SM breakout in main analyses (p. 3). Step 2 failure: Composite screen time includes TV, unspecified gaming, social media, and other media bundled without breakdown. Per §2.4, multi-device aggregated time including TV and unspecified gaming without separate SM/phone isolation fails IC2. Composite fails the "exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items" requirement of Step 2.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1: Paper reports separate social media trajectory analyses (5 groups) paired with anxiety/depression outcomes (Table 4, Figure 1b). Social media qualifies as phone/SM-specific variable. Results show significant associations (e.g., Group 3 "Increasing-then-Decreasing Use" β = 1.44, 95% CI [0.75, 2.12] for anxiety; p < 0.001) with anxiety/depression (individual-trait-level mental health outcomes). Other-side requirement met.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Numeric results reported for social media ↔ anxiety/depression relationship: linear regression coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values (Table 4). Main screen time groups also show significant associations with anxiety/depression (β ranging 0.55 to 1.44 for anxiety; p < 0.001)
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Inferential analysis: multivariable linear models with complex survey weights, group-based trajectory modeling, interaction terms examined. Numeric quantification throughout with p-values, CIs, and effect estimates reported.
FT_EC7_applies_C2_evidence: Longitudinal design (baseline to Year 3 follow-up). Screen time and social media analyzed as DV in all analyses (no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere in paper). No cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis present. All three conditions for FT-EC7 exclusion met.

BINDING rule: multi-device aggregated with TV and unspecified gaming → NOT_MET.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1 passes: "recreational screen time" is a phone/SM-specific variable (Table 1: 6.1±5.1 hours/day of screen use). FT_EC7_applies_C2_evidence: Cross-sectional carve-out applies. Although design is prospective (Year 2 exposure → Year 4 outcome), the analysis is fundamentally cross-sectional at Year 4 (screen time categories and CVD outcomes both measured at single waves). Cyberbullying is a peer-interaction exposure, not a phone/SM-specific behavioral measure. Other side is neural activation and mental health outcomes, which are individual-trait, but IC2 requires the OTHER side to be individual-trait when SM/phone is IV—here, SM/phone is not present at all.
FT_EC7_applies_C2_evidence: IC2 not met; EC7 evaluation deferred

title: Passive and social screen time in children with autism and in association with obesity.
FINAL_reason: all criteria MET; FT-EC7 not applicable
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline data from 10,842 participants across 21 sites (ages 9-10)
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Baseline data from 10,842 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognition Development Study; U.S. ABCD with 21 study sites.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis comparing autism vs. typically developing children on screen time and obesity
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional comparison of screen time patterns by autism status and gender, with propensity score matching and GLM analyses.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed original research published in Frontiers in Pediatrics
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Frontiers in Pediatrics.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Step 1 search successful: Four specific phone/SM variables with numeric estimates paired with obesity (individual-trait health outcome): (1) passive viewing (TV/movies/videos): hours/day with prevalence ratios; (2) video games: minutes/day with prevalence ratios; (3) social media/video chat: minutes/day with prevalence ratios (p=0.58 overall but significant by autism status); (4) texting: minutes/day with prevalence ratios (p=0.04). Other side is obesity status (binary health outcome = individual-trait-level)
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1 hit: paper reports separate estimates for phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcome. Passive viewing (TV/movies/videos), video games, social media/video chat, and texting hours per day each paired with obesity status (health outcome). Obesity prevalence increased significantly with passive screen time (p = 0.002), video games (p = 0.05), and texting (p = 0.04).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Multiple quantitative results: prevalence ratios with 95% confidence intervals, p-values, and stratified analyses by autism status and gender (Tables 3, 4, 5)
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Numeric results reported: prevalence ratios with 95% confidence intervals for each screen time category vs. reference. Example: passive screen time 4+ hours vs. <2 hours, PR = 2.15 (95% CI 1.40-3.31, p < 0.001).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric results for SM/phone-obesity associations: e.g., passive viewing p=0.002; video games p=0.05; texting p=0.04 overall; stratified by autism status with individual prevalence ratios
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric quantification: logistic and Poisson GLM models with prevalence ratios, 95% CIs, and p-values for SM/phone variables and obesity outcomes.
FT_EC7_applies_C1_evidence: Cross-sectional design (baseline data only); EC7 exclusion rule requires longitudinal design with SM as DV in every analysis and no SM-as-IV analysis. This paper is cross-sectional, so EC7 does not apply
FT_EC7_applies_C2_evidence: Cross-sectional design (baseline data only); EC7 requires longitudinal design. Cross-sectional carve-out applies: no longitudinal SM-as-DV-only pattern present.

title: ADHD and gaming addiction in adolescents: psychosocial mediators in the adolescent brain cognitive development study.

FT_IC1_C2_evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 2-4 data, approximately 7,260 participants, KSADS-COMP ADHD module and VGAQ administered
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal linear mixed-effects model and parallel mediation model examining ADHD diagnosis → psychosocial mediators → VGAQ scores
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models and parallel mediation model examining longitudinal association between ADHD diagnosis and VGAQ scores with nine psychosocial mediators
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Frontiers in Psychiatry, 2026 (published online 06 March 2026)
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article, Frontiers in Psychiatry, 2026
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Primary exposure variable is VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), a problematic-use scale, not a phone/SM-specific variable. VGAQ is classified as an SM/phone variable itself per binding clarification (§2.4, footnote 1), not as a qualifying outcome on the individual-trait side. No separate phone/SM modality variable (e.g., time on phone, time on SM) measured. Gaming addiction scale alone without explicit mobile/phone modality paired with a separately-measured SM/phone variable fails IC2 per binding rule.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Step 1: Paper analyzes VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), a generic video gaming addiction scale without explicit phone/mobile modality. Per binding clarification #2, gaming alone fails IC2 unless explicitly mobile/phone-based or paired with separately-measured phone/SM variable. No phone/SM variable with separate coefficient exists in this paper. VGAQ is the gaming variable, not a phone/SM variable.

title: Typologies of Family Functioning and 24-h Movement Behaviors.
